2: ki am qadosh atah l'YHWH Eloheicha uvcha bachar YHWH lihyot lo l'am segulah mikol ha'amim asher al-pnei ha'adamah (kî ʿam qādôš ʾattāh laYhwh ʾĕlōhêkā ûbĕkā bāḥar Yhwh lihyôt lô lĕʿam sĕgullāh mikkōl hāʿammîm ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê hāʾădāmāh): 'For you are a people holy to the LORD your God, and the LORD has chosen you to be a people for his treasured possession, out of all the peoples who are on the face of the earth.' Am qadosh (holy people) and am segulah (treasured possession/people of special property) are Deuteronomy's twin covenant-identity terms. Segulah (also Exodus 19:5; Deuteronomy 7:6; Deuteronomy 26:18; Malachi 3:17) derives from a root meaning private or personal property that one keeps close — it describes the intimate ownership-affection that distinguishes this people from all others. Peter applies both terms directly to the church (1 Peter 2:9-10): a holy nation, a people for YHWH's own possession. The dietary laws that follow in Deuteronomy 14:3-21 are grounded in this identity: what Israel eats is shaped by who they are — a people set apart for YHWH.
21: lo-tevashel gedi bachalev imo (lōʾ tĕbaššēl gĕdî baḥălēb ʾimmô): 'You shall not boil a young goat in its mother's milk.' This brief prohibition (repeated verbatim in Exodus 23:19; Deuteronomy 34:26) is the ancient scriptural basis for the rabbinic separation of meat and dairy foods (basar bechalav). Its precise rationale is debated: (1) a Canaanite fertility-ritual prohibition (though no such Ugaritic ritual is clearly attested); (2) a humanitarian principle against mixing life-food (milk) with death-food (meat) especially from the same animal; (3) a holiness-distinction principle consistent with Deuteronomy's separation-categories. The threefold repetition across Torah gives it canonical weight beyond its brevity. The NT never addresses it directly, but Paul's principle in 1 Cor 8-10 (idol food) applies similar logic: covenant identity shapes eating practices, and the offense to conscience may matter more than the intrinsic act.
1: banim atem laYHWH Eloheichem: 'You are children (bānîm) of the LORD your God.' The familial designation opens the purity laws: the food restrictions are grounded not in hygiene or arbitrary rules but in Israel's identity as YHWH's children (bānîm). The father-son relationship with YHWH is a Deuteronomic theme (Deuteronomy 1:31; Deuteronomy 8:5; Deuteronomy 32:6) that the NT applies to believers — 'you are all sons of God through faith' (Galatians 3:26). The mourning-rites prohibited (titgodedu, gashing yourselves; shim tusumu qorcha, shaving the front of the head for the dead) are Egyptian and Canaanite funerary practices that blurred the boundary between Israel and surrounding nations. The prohibition grounds: if you are YHWH's children, you do not mourn as those who have no covenant hope.
3: lo-tokhal kol-toeva: 'You shall not eat any detestable thing (tôʿēḇāh).' The single summary verse before the purity-list uses tôʿēḇāh (abomination, detestable thing) — the strongest category of violation in Deuteronomy's ethical vocabulary. The same word covers idolatry (Deuteronomy 7:25-26; Deuteronomy 13:15), sexual prohibitions (Leviticus 18:22,26-30), and unjust business practices (Deuteronomy 25:16). By leading the food-law section with this term, Deuteronomy frames dietary practice not as mere etiquette but as a matter of covenantal integrity: eating the wrong foods is categorically as serious as other covenant violations. The NT's Peter-Cornelius vision (Acts 10:14-15) directly addresses this tôʿēḇāh framework: 'What God has made clean, do not call common.'
4: zot habehemah asher tokhelon: 'These are the animals you may eat: the ox, the sheep, and the goat.' The permitted animal list begins with the three domestic herd/flock animals — shor (ox), seh kesavim (sheep), seh izzim (goat) — the primary sacrificial and food animals. Their permissibility is not incidental; these are the very animals used in sacrifice (Lev 1-5), establishing a structural connection between what Israel eats and what Israel offers: the animals that may be eaten are the animals that may be offered. The dietary laws and the sacrificial laws share the same list.
5: ha'ayyel vehatzvi vehayyachmur veha'aqqo vehadishon uteho vezamer: 'the deer, the gazelle, the roe deer, the wild goat, the ibex, the antelope, and the mountain sheep.' The list of wild game expands the permitted category to seven named species. Several of these are hapax legomena whose precise identification is uncertain (cf. the variety in English translations): yaḥmûr (roe deer or fallow deer), ʾaqqô (ibex?), dišôn (antelope?), tᵉô (wild ox?), zemer (mountain sheep? chamois?). The naming of seven species (a complete-number list) suggests the permission is comprehensive: all split-hooved, cud-chewing wild game is clean. The LXX's identification of these animals follows Hellenistic zoological categories rather than precise species correspondence.
6: vekhol behemah maphresat parsa shosa sheti ferasot maalat gerah: 'You may eat any animal that has completely split hooves and chews the cud.' The two-criteria system for clean land animals — pārāsāh (hoof) that is both maphresat (split, cleft) and šôsaʿat (completely split into two halves) plus maʿălat gērāh (brings up the cud, ruminates) — must both be present for an animal to be clean. The dual requirement prevents misidentification by single-criterion matching: the camel chews the cud but lacks the split hoof; the pig has the hoof but does not chew the cud. The Levitical parallel (Leviticus 11:2-8) is slightly fuller; Deuteronomy's summary form assumes the reader knows the Levitical tradition.
7: akh et-zeh lo tokhelon mimaalei hagerah umimmiphrisei haperisah hashesuyah: 'However, among those that chew the cud or have split hooves, these you shall not eat: the camel, the hare, and the rock badger.' The three borderline cases — gamal (camel), arnevet (hare), shaphān (rock badger/hyrax) — are animals that appear to meet one criterion but not the other. ki maʿaleh gerah hemmah ufarsah lo hiprisu (for they chew the cud but do not divide the hoof): the hare and rock badger are not true ruminants in modern taxonomy, but their chewing motion (grinding molars, caecotrophy in rabbits) presented as cud-chewing to ancient observers. The classification is phenomenological — based on observable behavior — rather than anatomical.
8: ve'et-haḥazir ki-maphris parsa hu velo yigar gerah tame hu lachem: 'And the pig — though it has split hooves, it does not chew the cud. It is unclean for you.' The pig (ḥăzîr) is mentioned last and with extra emphasis, perhaps because of its particular status in Canaanite and later Hellenistic culture. The pig's single-criterion status (hooves yes, cud no) makes it the paradigmatic unclean animal. In the Maccabean period (1 Macc Deuteronomy 1:47-63; 2 Macc Deuteronomy 6:18-7:42), forced pork consumption became the test-case for apostasy: refusing to eat pork was the defining act of Jewish identity maintenance under Antiochus IV. The pig-prohibition thus carried historical-martyrdom weight beyond its textual origin.
9: et-zeh tokhelon mikol asher bamayim kol-asher lo senapir vesquamah tokhelon: 'Of all creatures living in the water, you may eat those that have fins and scales.' The two criteria for clean aquatic life — sᵉnappîr (fins) and qasqeset (scales) — are simpler than the land-animal criteria. The positive formulation first ('you may eat whatever has fins and scales') before the negative prohibition makes the clean category the baseline. The criteria effectively permit fish and some shellfish while excluding others — but the precise scope is debated because ancient Israelite taxonomic knowledge of aquatic life was less developed than their knowledge of land animals.
10: vekhol asher ein lo senapir vesquamah lo tokhelon tame hu lachem: 'But whatever lacks fins or scales you shall not eat; it is unclean for you.' The negation of both criteria prohibits shellfish, crustaceans, eels, and catfish. ṭāmēʾ hûʾ lākem (it is unclean for you) — the personal pronoun lākem (for you) is significant: the uncleanness is relational, not absolute. The animal is not intrinsically corrupt; it is unsuitable for Israel because of Israel's covenant status as a holy people. The NT's 'clean all foods' declaration (Mark 7:19; Acts 10:15; Romans 14:14) removes the lākem restriction by extending the covenant community beyond ethnic Israel.
11: kol-tzippor tehorah tokhelon: 'You may eat any clean bird.' The positive statement for birds is brief because the list immediately follows with the prohibited species (Deuteronomy 14:12-18). The clean-bird category is defined by exclusion rather than positive criteria. The absence of anatomical criteria for birds (unlike the hoof/cud system for land animals) suggests that identification relied on traditional knowledge and specific prohibitory lists rather than a systematic rule. The Mishnaic tractate Hullin develops a four-sign system for birds (crops, extra toe, peelable gizzard, egg-catching behavior), but this is later rabbinic development.
12: ve'elleh asher lo tokhelon mehem hannesher vehaperes veha'ozniyyah: 'These are the birds you shall not eat: the eagle, the bearded vulture (peres), and the black vulture (ʾozniyyāh).' The prohibited bird list begins with the large raptors — nesher (eagle or griffon vulture), peres (bearded vulture/lammergeier), ʾozniyyāh (black vulture or osprey). These are all carrion-feeding or large predatory birds. The ecological principle seems to be that birds of prey that tear and consume blood are unsuitable — consistent with the blood-prohibition that underlies much of the kashrut system.
13: vehara'ah ve'et-ha'ayya vehadayyah leminam: 'the kite and any kind of falcon.' rāʾāh (kite), ʾayyāh (falcon or kite species), dayyāh (kite or falcon, Leviticus 11:14) — the phrase lemînāh (according to its kind) extends each prohibition to all species of the genus. The same extension-phrase (lemîn) is used in Genesis 1's creation account for the multiplication of living creatures according to their kinds — Deuteronomy's food laws use creation-vocabulary to organize the animal world.
14: ve'et kol-orev lemino: 'every kind of raven.' The raven (ʿōrēḇ) is prohibited with its entire genus (lᵉmînô). Ravens and crows are omnivores that feed on carrion — consistent with the predator/carrion-feeder pattern of the prohibited bird list. The raven appears in biblical narrative at significant moments: sent out by Noah from the ark (Genesis 8:7) and feeding Elijah at the brook Cherith (1 Kings 17:6) — a divine use of an unclean bird for a clean purpose, demonstrating YHWH's sovereign freedom over the categories he has established.
15: ve'et-ha'astityah vehat-tes vehashahal ve'et-hanets leminehu: 'the ostrich, the nighthawk, the sea gull, and every kind of hawk.' The bat-yaʿănāh (ostrich, literally 'daughter of howling' or 'daughter of greed') appears in Leviticus 11:16 as well. The ostrich's inclusion in the prohibited list is consistent with its behavior as a scavenger and its consumption of various non-food materials. The lemîn extension again covers all species of each type, making the prohibitory list comprehensive across genera.
16: ha-kos vehashalakh vehayanshup: 'the little owl, the screech owl, and the barn owl.' The three owl species (precise identifications uncertain — kôs, šālaḵ, yansûp) are nocturnal predators. The owl's association with desolation and ruins in the prophets (Isaiah 34:11,13-15; Jeremiah 50:39; Zephaniah 2:14) may reflect its status as an unclean bird dwelling in destroyed places — a habitat that symbolizes divine judgment. The banned birds tend to be associated with death (carrion-feeders), predatory violence (raptors), or desolation (owls).
17: vehaqa'at ve'et-harakham ve'et-hashalaḵ: 'the pelican, the carrion vulture (rāhām), and the cormorant (šālaḵ).' qāʾāt (pelican or owl species), rāhām (osprey or Egyptian vulture — related to raḥam, compassion/love, an ironic bird-name), šālaḵ (gannet or cormorant). These are aquatic birds that pursue fish rather than live on land — adding a dimension to the prohibited birds: not only carrion-feeders and raptors but fish-eating water-birds are also excluded. The fish-catching birds stand at the intersection of clean aquatic prey and unclean bird predation.
18: vehaḥasidah veha'anafah lemina vehadukiphath vehaat'alleph: 'the stork, any kind of heron, the hoopoe, and the bat.' ḥăsîdāh (stork) — the name derives from ḥesed (steadfast love/kindness), perhaps because storks were observed caring attentively for their young. The irony that the ḥăsîdāh (kindness-bird) is unclean was noticed in rabbinic literature (b. Hullin 63a). ʿăṭallēp (bat) — the bat is technically a mammal but classified with the birds in the ancient taxonomy (as in Leviticus 11:19); it concludes the bird list with the most anomalous creature, one that flies but has no feathers.
19: vekhol-shretz ha'of tame hu lachem lo ye'akhelu: 'All winged insects (šereṣ hāʿôp) are unclean for you; they shall not be eaten.' The šereṣ category (swarming creatures) covers insects and small flying creatures generally. Leviticus 11:20-23 qualifies this: winged insects that go on all fours are unclean, but those with jointed legs for leaping (locusts, crickets, grasshoppers) are clean. Deuteronomy's summary form does not include the locust exception, which is assumed from the Levitical tradition. John the Baptist's diet of locusts and wild honey (Matthew 3:4; Mark 1:6) was fully within the permitted-insect exception, placing him squarely within Israel's kashrut framework.
20: kol-of tahor tokhelon: 'But all clean birds you may eat.' The brief summary permission (echoing Deuteronomy 14:11) closes the prohibited-bird section by restating the positive category. The repetition brackets the prohibition list (Deuteronomy 14:11 → prohibited list → Deuteronomy 14:20) with positive permission statements — Deuteronomy's rhetorical pattern of sandwiching prohibitions within affirmations of what is allowed, reflecting the covenant's positive framework: the restrictions exist within a broader grant of freedom.
22: aser teaser et kol-tevuat zarecha hayotzeh hasadeh shana shana: 'You shall tithe (ʿaśśēr tᵉʿaśśēr) all the yield of your seed that comes from the field each year.' The doubled verb ʿaśśēr tᵉʿaśśēr (tithe you shall tithe) — the Piel infinitive absolute + finite verb — is the Hebrew emphasis construction, indicating the strongest possible obligation: you must certainly tithe. māʿăśēr (tithe, ten-percent) — the practice of giving a tenth to religious institutions has ancient Near Eastern precedent (tribute to temples, to kings). Deuteronomy's tithe is specifically designated for the sanctuary feast at the chosen place, with provision for the Levite (Deuteronomy 14:27) and the poor every third year (Deuteronomy 14:28-29). The system distributes the agricultural surplus through worship and social solidarity.
23: ve'akhalita lifnei YHWH Eloheicha bemakom asher yivchar leshakem shemo sham: 'And in the presence of YHWH your God, at the place he will choose to make his name dwell, you shall eat the tithe of your grain, wine, and oil, and the firstborn of your herd and flock.' The tithe is to be eaten in YHWH's presence (lipnê YHWH) — a communal feast before YHWH at the central sanctuary, not merely a tax payment. lᵉmaʿan tilmad lᵉyārᵉʾāh ʾet-YHWH Eloheycha kol-hayyamim (so that you may learn to fear the LORD your God always) — the feast's purpose is formative: annual eating of the tithe at the sanctuary is an annual education in YHWH-fear, a structured liturgical pedagogy that sustains covenant orientation through repeated practice.
24: vekhiy-yirbeh mimkha haderekh ki lo tukhal se'eto: 'But if the distance is too great for you to carry the tithe.' The tithe law includes a practical accommodation for distance: if the central sanctuary is too far, the tithe may be converted to money (kesep). This kesef tiqquph (money conversion) provision anticipates the reality of scattered settlement in Canaan after the conquest. The conversion is not a debasement of the requirement but an adaptation: the purpose (feast before YHWH) is maintained; the form (carrying physical produce vs. carrying money and buying at the destination) is adjusted for practical necessity.
25: venatatah bakkesef vetzarerta hakesef beyadkha: 'then you shall exchange the tithe for money, and take the money in hand to the place that the LORD your God will choose.' wᵉṣārartā hakkesep bᵉyāḏᵉkā (wrap/tie the money in your hand) — the idiom means to carry money safely bundled. The practical detail — wrap it and take it — grounds the religious obligation in ordinary commercial reality. Deuteronomy consistently moves between the transcendent (YHWH's holiness) and the mundane (practical livestock management, commercial exchange) without embarrassment: the covenant governs all of life, including how to carry money to the festival.
26: unatata hakesef bekhol asher te'aveh nafshkha babaqar uvatsaon uvayayin uvashekhar: 'and spend the money for whatever you desire — cattle, sheep, wine, strong drink, or whatever you want — and you shall eat it before YHWH your God and rejoice.' wᵉšāmḥtā ʾattāh ûḇêtekā (you shall rejoice, you and your household) — the tithe feast is explicitly joyful. The permission for šēḵār (strong drink, fermented beverage) — often prohibited or restricted — is here explicitly permitted for the tithe feast. The feast is an expression of YHWH's abundance: eat what you desire, rejoice before YHWH. The NT counterpart is the messianic banquet (Luke 14:15-24; Revelation 19:9), the ultimate feast-before-YHWH to which all these communal meals pointed.
27: vehaLevi asher bishareykha lo ta'azvennu ki ein lo helek venachalah imakh: 'And do not neglect (lōʾ taʿazĕḇennû) the Levite who lives in your towns, for he has no portion or inheritance among you.' The Levite's landlessness is the structural rationale for the tithe provision. ʾên lô ḥēleq wᵉnaḥălāh ʿimmāk (he has no portion or inheritance with you) — the Levites received no territorial allotment in the Canaan distribution (Numbers 18:20-24; Deuteronomy 18:1-2) because YHWH himself is their inheritance. Their economic sustenance therefore depends on the tithe system. The verb taʿazĕḇ (to abandon, forsake) is a strong term: neglecting the Levite is the covenant equivalent of abandoning a dependent member of the covenant community.
28: miqqets shalosh shanim totzi et-kol-maʿasar tevuatekha bashanah hahi vehinikhta bishearekha: 'At the end of every three years you shall bring out all the tithe of your produce from that year and store it within your towns.' The third-year tithe (maʿasar shanim shalosh) — distinct from the regular annual tithe — is stored locally rather than taken to the central sanctuary. This local-storage tithe creates a community welfare reserve, accessible throughout the town for the Levite, the resident alien, the fatherless, and the widow (Deuteronomy 14:29). The three-year cycle aligns with the Sabbatical year system (every seventh year there is a full release, Deuteronomy 15) and creates a multi-layered provision infrastructure.
29: uvaa haLevi ki ein lo helek venachalah imakh veha-ger vehayatom vehaalmanah asher bishareykha veakhelu vesaveyu: 'so that the Levite — who has no portion or inheritance among you — and the resident foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow who are in your towns may come and eat and be satisfied.' The beneficiary list — Levite, gēr (resident alien), yātôm (fatherless), ʾalmānāh (widow) — is Deuteronomy's standard quadruplet of the covenant's economically vulnerable. Their needs are structurally built into the tithe calendar: the provision for the poor is not charity but covenant obligation, scheduled and institutionalized. lᵉmaʿan yᵉḇārᵉkᵉkā YHWH Eloheycha bᵉkol-maʿăśēh yāḏᵉkā ʾăšer taʿăśeh (so that YHWH your God may bless you in all the work of your hands) — generosity to the vulnerable is the condition for YHWH's blessing, making the welfare system a covenant-economic investment, not merely humanitarian sentiment.
